nativeRange.habitat: Anubias Frazeri is a cultivated hybrid with no wild population of its own. Its parent species — Anubias barteri and Anubias heterophylla (sometimes listed as Anubias congensis) — are native to shaded streams, river margins, and forest swamps in tropical West and Central Africa, where they grow attached to wood and rocks in both submerged and partially emergent conditions.

decorPreferences: rocks and hardscape (e.g. Seiryu, Ohko Dragon Stone)
shaded placement to reduce leaf algae
rhizome must remain above the substrate

goodWith: most African cichlids (tough leaves resist nibbling)

summary: Anubias Frazeri is a mid-sized cultivated hybrid — likely a cross of Anubias barteri and Anubias heterophylla — with broad, pointed leaves and a reputation as one of the more beginner-friendly Anubias available. The rhizome must be attached to hardscape, never buried.
careNotes:
Anubias Frazeri is a cultivated hybrid widely traded in the aquarium hobby, most commonly under the names 'Anubias sp. Frazeri' or simply 'Anubias Frazeri'. It is rumored to have been created by hobbyist Ed Frazer, after whom it is named. Mature plants reach roughly 15–35 cm tall, with broad, pointed, lance-to-arrow-shaped leaves that are tougher and more substantial than those of smaller cultivars like Anubias nana 'Petite'. Its size and form make it best suited to midground placement, though taller specimens can serve as background plants in nano and small community tanks.

Care follows the same principles as other Anubias. Low to medium lighting is preferred — excess light combined with the plant's naturally slow growth invites algae to colonize the leaves. CO2 injection is optional rather than required; it speeds growth somewhat but is not necessary for the plant to thrive. Growth is slow regardless of conditions.

DO NOT BURY THE RHIZOME. The rhizome — the thick horizontal stem from which the leaves and roots emerge — must remain above the substrate at all times. Burying it reliably causes it to rot and kills the plant. Instead, attach Anubias Frazeri to driftwood, rocks, or other hardscape using cotton thread, fishing line, or a small dab of cyanoacrylate (super) glue; the roots will anchor on their own within a few weeks. Because the plant feeds primarily from the water column rather than from substrate nutrients, liquid fertilization tends to be more effective than substrate fertilization.
breedingNotes: Anubias Frazeri is propagated by rhizome division. Using a clean, sharp blade, cut a healthy rhizome into sections and ensure each section retains at least 2–3 healthy leaves and some attached roots. Re-attach each section to hardscape — do not bury the cut rhizome. New roots typically establish within about two weeks. Like other Anubias, the plant can flower underwater, producing a small spathe-and-spadix inflorescence typical of the Araceae family. As a hybrid, however, it does not come reliably true from seed and is propagated only vegetatively.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed: midground-to-background placement, low-to-moderate lighting, slow growth, CO2 not required, easy care, broad arrow-shaped leaves, rhizome-must-not-be-buried instruction, temperature 70–82 °F (21–28 °C).

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Used as effective secondary after the designated secondary (Buce Plant) returned HTTP 404. Confirmed: hybrid parentage stated as Anubias barteri x A. heterophylla, family Araceae, temperature 72–80 °F (22–27 °C), pH 5.5–9.0 tolerance, low-to-moderate light, CO2 not strongly responsive, slow growth, midground placement, rhizome-must-not-be-fully-buried, easy difficulty, narrower/more pointed leaves than other Anubias.

additional.name: Light Fish
additional.notes: Third source for triangulation. Confirmed: 'Anubias sp. Frazeri' naming convention, hybrid origin (suggested Anubias congensis x A. barteri, rumored to be a hobbyist cross by Ed Frazer), temperature 72–82 °F (22–28 °C), pH 6.5–7.2, GH 2–15 dGH, KH 3–8 dKH, height 15–18 cm, low light tolerance, CO2 optional, rhizome-must-not-be-buried, easy difficulty, propagation by rhizome cutting with root establishment in about 2 weeks.
